stories.headline: Two Denver men shot and killed while confronting car thieves in separate incidents
stories.summary:
Two men were fatally shot in Denver over an eight-day span while attempting to stop people from breaking into or stealing their vehicles, the Denver Police Department announced Thursday. The first killing occurred at 1:15 a.m. on June 24 in the 15000 block of East Olmstead Place; the second took place just before midnight Tuesday in the 4300 block of Pecos Street. Neither victim was armed, according to police spokesman Doug Schepman.

Investigators have not yet determined whether the two incidents are connected, but the short timeframe and similar circumstances prompted police to issue a public warning. Chief Ron Thomas urged residents not to confront suspects during vehicle break-ins or theft attempts, saying people should call 911 and wait for officers. Schepman said investigators have made progress on both cases but have not yet identified suspects.

The killings are the latest in a pattern of violence tied to residents confronting would-be thieves in the Denver metro area. Notable prior incidents include the 2023 fatal shooting of 12-year-old Elias Armstrong by a man who tracked his stolen car, and a 2025 murder conviction of an Aurora man who chased and shot two teenagers he suspected of trying to steal his wife's vehicle.

Police stressed the warning is not intended to shame the victims but to raise public awareness about the dangers of directly engaging suspects. The identities of the two men killed have not yet been released by the Office of the Medical Examiner.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres wildfire surges past 50,000 acres, remains 0% contained as new evacuations ordered
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres Fire grew to 50,187 acres by Thursday, up from 35,468 acres earlier that morning, making it the state's largest active fire and the nation's top fire priority, according to incident commanders. The fire remains at 0 percent containment, with more than 300 firefighters on the ground including emergency teams from Alaska and other states. Alaska Complex Incident Management Team 1 assumed command of the operation Thursday morning.

The communities of Wetmore and Colorado City were ordered to evacuate Wednesday as the fire pushed north and east, joining previously evacuated areas of Beulah, Rye and San Isabel. The National Weather Service issued red-flag weather warnings for Pueblo and Custer counties through Friday, with southwest winds of 20 to 25 mph expected to drive continued extreme fire behavior. Incident commanders warned further growth is inevitable under current conditions.

Governor Jared Polis has declared a disaster emergency for the Aspen Acres Fire along with four other major fires burning across the state. The American Red Cross of Southern Colorado has opened a disaster assistance center for displaced residents. Officials say the fire is approximately two miles from the popular Bishop Castle attraction and seven miles from Wetmore.

The fire's rapid growth comes amid a historically dangerous fire season fueled by record-low winter snowpack, severe drought and critically dry fuels. Red-flag conditions are forecast to persist through the Fourth of July holiday weekend, with firefighting aircraft occasionally grounded by erratic wind gusts.

stories.headline: Nine wildfires burning across Colorado as Fourth of July weekend approaches
stories.summary:
Nine active wildfires continue to burn across Colorado heading into the Fourth of July holiday weekend, with numerous mandatory evacuations and pre-evacuation notices in place across multiple counties. The fires span from the Western Slope to southern Colorado, stretching firefighting resources and drawing national support. Every county west of the I-25 corridor is currently under fire restrictions.

Among the most active fires, the Ferris Fire — which merged with the Doe Canyon and Far Draw fires — has moved toward the Dolores River Canyon, approximately three miles north of Bradfield Bridge. The Gold Mountain Fire on the Western Slope has grown to more than 18,000 acres, and the Snyder Fire along the Colorado-Utah border has seen minimal growth with containment slowly increasing. The Willow Fire west of Leadville saw increased activity Tuesday due to warm, dry conditions and winds.

Red-flag warnings remain in effect for much of Colorado through the holiday weekend, with forecasters predicting temperatures in the 90s, relative humidity dropping below 5 percent in some areas, and wind gusts up to 45 mph. Firefighting aircraft have occasionally been grounded by erratic winds, further hampering containment efforts. The Gold Mountain Fire has prompted closure of Buckhorn Lakes near Montrose and the Cimarron State Wildlife Area.

State officials and fire chiefs are urging the public to strictly follow all fire bans and fireworks restrictions ahead of Independence Day. The Colorado Department of Transportation has paused all statewide road construction and is urging mountain-bound travelers to leave early to avoid peak holiday traffic.

stories.headline: Metro Denver fireworks bans, fire restrictions and canceled shows ahead of Fourth of July
stories.summary:
A wave of fireworks bans and show cancellations is sweeping the Denver metro and surrounding areas as ongoing wildfires and Stage 2 fire restrictions reshape Independence Day plans. Douglas County canceled all county-sponsored fireworks shows Thursday, and Jefferson County banned both the use and sale of all fireworks in unincorporated areas after upgrading to Stage 2 restrictions. Woodland Park, Cripple Creek and Ouray have also canceled their fireworks shows.

Within the City and County of Denver, all fireworks — including sparklers and snakes — are prohibited under the Denver Municipal Code regardless of fire conditions. Violations can result in fines up to $999 and up to one year in jail. Denver's annual Civic Center Park celebration has been canceled due to construction, though Sunset Park will host a screening of 'National Treasure' on July 3 followed by a drone show, and Coors Field will feature fireworks after Rockies games on July 3 and July 4.

Statewide, most fireworks that explode or leave the ground are illegal for personal use in Colorado, including those purchased in other states such as Wyoming. The permitted types — sparklers, smoke bombs, fountains and snakes — are also now banned in many jurisdictions under local fire restrictions. Penalties for possessing banned fireworks under state law can include up to a 10-day jail sentence and a $300 fine.

Denver officials are asking residents not to call 911 to report illegal fireworks unless there is an injury or fire threatening life or property. Residents may instead call the city's dedicated fireworks line at 720-913-2059 or use the Sunny chatbot on DenverGov.org.

stories.headline: Aldi files plans for first two Colorado store locations near DIA and in Central Park
stories.summary:
Aldi has submitted formal plans to the City of Denver for its first two Colorado grocery stores, advancing the German discount chain's long-anticipated expansion into the state. One proposed location is a 19,957-square-foot store at 18453 E. 57th Ave. near Denver International Airport, to be built on a vacant 2.37-acre site at the corner of Tower Road and East 57th Avenue. A second 19,432-square-foot store is planned at 9111 E. 40th Ave. in Denver's Central Park neighborhood, near an existing QuikTrip, Wendy's and Denver's first Cracker Barrel.

The filings were submitted Wednesday by Michigan-based Atwell LLC on behalf of Aldi Real Estate Inc. The DIA-area store would include beer and wine sales and 90 parking spaces; the Central Park location would have 109 spaces. Both are planned as single-story buildings.

The permit applications represent the next concrete step in Aldi's announced plan to open more than 50 stores across the Denver and Colorado Springs areas. A new distribution center in Aurora is expected to open in 2029 and is projected to create hundreds of jobs. Aldi U.S. CEO Atty McGrath said in January the expansion reflects the chain's commitment to delivering affordable groceries to new markets.

Founded in Germany in 1913, Aldi opened its first U.S. store in Iowa in 1976 and currently operates thousands of stores across more than 10 countries. The company is targeting 3,200 U.S. locations by the end of 2028. Representatives from Aldi and Atwell did not respond to requests for comment.

stories.headline: Pedestrian killed after being struck by jockey truck at north Denver industrial yard
stories.summary:
A pedestrian was struck and killed by a truck at a north Denver industrial yard early Thursday morning, according to Denver Police Department officials. Officers responded to the 4700 block of East 50th Avenue shortly before 5 a.m. after a report of a vehicle-pedestrian crash. The pedestrian was transported to a hospital and later pronounced dead; their identity has not been released.

Investigators determined the driver was operating a jockey truck — a vehicle designed to move large trailers — and did not see the person walking in the yard. The driver was charged with careless driving resulting in death. No additional suspects or witnesses have been publicly identified.

stories.headline: Missing Arapahoe County senior found dead in Denver creek after weeks-long search
stories.summary:
A 63-year-old man who was reported missing last month from an Arapahoe County senior living facility has been found dead in Denver, the Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office announced Wednesday. Kevin Bailey, an Air Force veteran, was reported missing June 12 after leaving the Woodland Estates Senior Living Center at 2500 S. Roslyn St. He told staff he wanted to ride the bus and visit friends, and later told family he was looking for work.

Deputies, the Arapahoe Rescue Patrol and two search-and-rescue dogs searched Denver's Central Park area for two days in late June. A man's body was discovered in a creek on June 26, and the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner subsequently identified the remains as Bailey. The cause and manner of death have not been publicly released.

stories.headline: Santa Fe Drive in Denver's Overland neighborhood to close for weekend bridge installation in July
stories.summary:
South Santa Fe Drive in Denver's Overland neighborhood will be closed in both directions between Evans and Florida avenues for a weekend next month as crews use cranes to install the main spans of a new pedestrian bridge. The closure is scheduled from midnight Saturday, July 11, through 5 a.m. Monday, July 13. If weather causes delays, the closure will shift to July 18 through 20.

The new bridge, funded by the Elevate Denver Bond program, has been more than a decade in development. It will span approximately 370 feet of roadway and adjacent rail tracks, stand 30 feet above the ground and measure roughly 12 feet wide. Each of the two spans to be set during the July weekend weighs approximately 215,000 pounds.

Northbound traffic on Santa Fe Drive will detour east on Evans Avenue, north on Broadway and west on Mississippi Avenue before returning to northbound Santa Fe. Southbound drivers will be rerouted west on Florida Avenue, south on Platte River Drive and east on Evans Avenue. The West Evans Avenue on-ramp to northbound Santa Fe will close at noon Friday, July 10, reopening July 13.

The southbound Santa Fe Drive off-ramp to West Evans Avenue will remain closed until September 11 while crews complete sidewalk and concrete work. The Department of Transportation and Infrastructure oversees the project.

stories.headline: Wildfire smoke triggers air quality alerts along Denver Front Range as holiday weekend nears
stories.summary:
An Air Quality Health Advisory remains in effect along the Front Range due to wildfire smoke and elevated ozone levels, affecting Denver and much of the surrounding metro area. Temperatures are forecast to reach the low to mid 90s in Denver through Friday before a mostly dry Fourth of July. Red-flag warnings have been issued for much of Colorado through the holiday weekend, with wind gusts up to 45 mph anticipated.

Denver7 meteorologists warn conditions will remain hot, dry and smoky well into next week, with only minimal chance of meaningful rainfall. A slight increase in humidity and potential evening showers are possible Sunday, though forecasters caution the threat of dry lightning could spark additional fires rather than provide meaningful relief. Residents with respiratory conditions are advised to limit outdoor exposure during smoke events.

The National Weather Service describes the forecast as nearly identical day to day, with hazy sunshine and smoke sticking around the urban corridor. Skies are expected to be clear of storm activity through July 4 but wildfire smoke is likely to remain a persistent feature over the summer.

stories.headline: Broncos announce all 2026 draft picks signed ahead of training camp opening
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have signed their entire 2026 NFL Draft class ahead of the team's training camp, which is scheduled to open July 28. The team previously announced 14 practices will be open to the public beginning July 31. With all rookies under contract, the full roster will be available for the start of camp.

The Broncos are entering their second consecutive season after finishing with the third-fewest points allowed in the NFL last year. Quarterback Bo Nix is returning from an undisclosed issue and has been practicing, with the team expressing confidence in his readiness heading into his third season. Offensive lineman Mike McGlinchey has also been highlighted as a key returning veteran.

stories.headline: Tim Hardaway Jr. departs Denver Nuggets, agrees to join Miami Heat in NBA free agency
stories.summary:
Tim Hardaway Jr. is leaving the Denver Nuggets, agreeing to sign with the Miami Heat as NBA free agency opened, according to reports. The veteran guard was part of Denver's roster last season and his departure gives the Nuggets additional roster flexibility as the organization navigates an active offseason. Financial terms of the deal with Miami were not immediately disclosed.

The Nuggets entered free agency with several roster questions, including ongoing trade interest around forward Aaron Gordon and the team's pursuit of additional star-level talent to complement two-time MVP Nikola Jokic. The club has also been connected to LeBron James in early free agency conversations. Hardaway's exit further underscores the significant roster turnover Denver faces heading into next season.

stories.headline: Colorado Buffaloes tennis coach Anthony Pham signs contract extension through 2029
stories.summary:
University of Colorado tennis coach Anthony Pham has signed a contract extension that will keep him in Boulder through the 2029 season, athletics director Fernando Lovo announced Wednesday. Pham, who has led the Buffaloes program for five seasons, guided CU to a 10-13 record last year, finishing 75th nationally. The team's highlight was an upset of then-No. 28 Oklahoma State, the program's highest-ranked win since 2023.

'Coach Pham has been a tremendous leader for our tennis student-athletes, whose teams are consistently resilient, tough, and incredibly prepared,' Lovo said in a statement. The extension signals the athletics department's confidence in Pham's ability to continue developing the program within the Big 12 Conference.

joke.joke: Why do they call it a 'building'? It looks like they're finished. Why isn't it a 'built'?

topStory.headline: Russia launches 11-hour missile and drone barrage on Kyiv, killing at least 22 civilians
topStory.summary:
Russia struck Kyiv with one of its largest attacks of the war overnight into Thursday, firing 74 missiles and 496 drones at the Ukrainian capital over an 11-hour period. At least 22 civilians were killed and scores more injured, including at least two children, as emergency crews spent the day digging through the rubble of collapsed and burned apartment buildings across the city.

Ukrainian air defenses intercepted 48 missiles and 476 drones, but 25 ballistic missiles and 12 drones broke through, striking 33 locations. Among the sites damaged were a nine-story residential building where a single missile destroyed 64 apartments, an ambulance station, a scientific institute, a hotel, and an energy infrastructure facility operated by private company DTEK. An inventory warehouse belonging to Ukrainian publishing house BookChef was destroyed, with approximately 800,000 books lost.

More than 50,000 residents sheltered in Kyiv's subway stations during the attack, with many having preemptively sought cover after President Volodymyr Zelenskyy warned hours earlier that a massive Russian strike was imminent. Kyiv Mayor Vitali Klitschko declared Friday a day of mourning. Zelenskyy vowed retaliation, saying Ukrainian forces would 'definitely' respond to what he called a deliberate assault on civilians and residential areas.

Russia's Defense Ministry said the bombardment was carried out in retaliation for a Ukrainian 40-day blitz of long-range drone strikes targeting Russian oil refineries and supply infrastructure, which Moscow said has caused severe fuel shortages. Ukraine has increasingly used advances in drone technology to strike targets deep inside Russian territory, including on the Crimean Peninsula, disrupting supply lines and slowing Russian battlefield progress, according to analysts and Western officials.

The attack is among the deadliest single strikes on Kyiv since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. The war remains the deadliest conflict in Europe since World War II, and Thursday's strike underscores the continued Russian willingness to target civilian population centers as both sides escalate long-range strike campaigns against each other's territory.
